article_title: ClickFix Removes Your Background but Leaves the Malware
core_thesis: This article details a sophisticated multi-stage malware campaign dubbed 'BackgroundFix' that uses a fake online background-removal tool as a ClickFix lure. The attack chain delivers CastleLoader, which then drops NetSupport RAT and a custom .NET stealer (CastleStealer). The analysis provides a deep technical breakdown of each stage, including the use of finger.exe for initial payload delivery, Python-based downloaders, shellcode with creative evasion techniques (e.g., ReplaceTextW hook abuse), and a reflective PE loader. The article emphasizes that while the chain is complex, it can be disrupted by basic security controls like disabling the Run dialog, blocking finger.exe traffic, and keeping browsers updated.
detailed_summary: The article begins by describing the social engineering lure: a fake website that appears to remove image backgrounds. When the user clicks 'I'm not a robot', a malicious command is copied to the clipboard via document.execCommand('copy'), and the page sends telemetry to the attacker. The command uses finger.exe (a legacy Windows tool) to fetch a payload from a remote finger daemon. The finger server response contains a batch script that downloads a legitimate Python embeddable distribution from python.org, renames the Python executable to a random name, and executes a base64/zlib/UTF-32 encoded Python downloader. This downloader fetches a second Python script that uses Cyrillic character substitution to obfuscate a ctypes shellcode loader. The shellcode (Shellcode #1) is XOR-encrypted with a 16-byte key, uses a PEB-walking API resolver with djb2 hashing, and downloads a second-stage RC4-encrypted shellcode. The decryption uses the first 64 bytes of the download as the RC4 key. Shellcode #1 then executes the decrypted payload via a creative technique: it calls ReplaceTextW (a Windows dialog API) with a hook function pointer pointing to the decrypted payload, evading EDR hooks on VirtualProtect. Shellcode #2 is a 32-bit reflective PE loader that maps an embedded PE into memory, calling NtAllocateVirtualMemory directly to bypass VirtualAlloc hooks, and rewrites the PEB to make the embedded PE appear as the main executable. The embedded PE is CastleLoader, which uses API hashing (alternating-step algorithm) and XOR-encrypted stack strings for configuration. CastleLoader communicates with its C2 using ChaCha20 encryption, supports 14 launch methods (including process hollowing, APC injection, and PowerShell wrappers), and can execute tasks such as dropping NetSupport RAT and a .NET stealer. The stealer (CastleStealer) is a .NET assembly that uses a custom string decryption algorithm, performs anti-analysis (skips Russian locale), collects host fingerprinting via WMI, and steals browser credentials (Chromium Login Data, cookies, wallet extensions via IndexedDB), Discord tokens, and Telegram sessions. It uses the Restart Manager API to handle locked files and includes a process hollowing primitive targeting PowerShell. The article concludes with detection recommendations (YARA rule) and mitigation advice, including disabling the Run dialog, blocking TCP port 79 (finger), and keeping Chromium browsers updated to leverage App-Bound Encryption.

entities.name: CastleStealer
entities.role: Custom .NET information stealer targeting browser credentials, crypto wallets, and Telegram

entities.name: NetSupport RAT
entities.role: Remote access trojan dropped by CastleLoader for hands-on keyboard access

entities.name: BackgroundFix
entities.role: ClickFix lure using fake background-removal websites

entities.role: CastleLoader C2 and stage-2 payload hosting

entities.name: cheeshomireciple[.]com
entities.role: Finger daemon C2 for initial payload delivery

entities.name: Validin
entities.role: Threat intelligence platform used to pivot on related domains

key_data_points: The ClickFix lure uses a fake background-removal website; when the user checks 'I'm not a robot', a malicious command is copied to the clipboard via document.execCommand('copy') and the page sends a beacon to log-checkbox.php for attacker telemetry. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: demonstrates a social engineering technique that bypasses traditional email/attachment-based delivery.)

The initial payload is delivered via finger.exe (Windows legacy tool) querying a remote finger daemon at cheeshomireciple[.]com; the response includes a batch script that downloads a legitimate Python embeddable distribution from python.org. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: finger.exe is often overlooked; can be used as a living-off-the-land binary for initial access.)

CastleLoader uses a bring-your-own-interpreter (BYOI) approach: it downloads Python from python.org, renames the executable to a random name (e.g., 29693877.exe), and executes a Python downloader that fetches a second-stage shellcode. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: BYOI evades application whitelisting and signature-based detection.)

Shellcode #1 uses a PEB-walking API resolver with djb2 hashing and executes the next stage via ReplaceTextW hook abuse, bypassing EDR hooks on VirtualProtect. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this evasion technique can be weaponized in custom shellcode to avoid detection.)

Shellcode #2 is a reflective PE loader that calls NtAllocateVirtualMemory directly (bypassing VirtualAlloc hooks) and rewrites the PEB to make the embedded PE appear as the main executable. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: direct syscalls and PEB manipulation are key techniques for evading user-mode hooks.)

CastleLoader supports 14 launch methods, including process hollowing, APC injection, and PowerShell wrappers; method 4 (regsvr32.exe) is broken due to a typo (regsrv32.exe). (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding these methods helps in developing custom loaders and identifying weaknesses in adversary tooling.)

CastleStealer uses a custom string decryption algorithm with a 256-byte S-box and per-byte keystream; it performs anti-analysis by checking for Russian locale (ru-RU) and exits if found. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this is a common evasion technique; can be used to avoid targeting certain regions.)

CastleStealer steals Chromium browser credentials via DPAPI-then-AES (legacy path), wallet extensions via IndexedDB, Discord tokens, and Telegram sessions by copying the tdata directory. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding these extraction methods helps in developing or defending against credential theft.)

The stealer uses the Restart Manager API (RmStartSession, etc.) to handle locked files instead of killing processes, reducing telemetry. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this is a more stealthy approach to file access; can be adopted in custom tools.)

The article provides IOCs including hashes, domains, IPs, and campaign UUIDs for detection. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: these IOCs can be used to test detection capabilities or as indicators of compromise in simulations.)
methodology_steps: Step 1: The user visits a fake background-removal website and uploads an image. The image is not actually processed; it stays local.
Step 2: When the user clicks 'I'm not a robot', the page copies a malicious command to the clipboard using document.execCommand('copy') and sends a beacon to log-checkbox.php.
Step 3: The user is instructed to press Win+R, paste the command, and press Enter. The command uses finger.exe to query a remote finger daemon (cheeshomireciple[.]com).

Step 4: The finger daemon responds with a batch script that: sets random variable names, kills explorer.exe, downloads Python embeddable from python.org using curl, extracts it with tar, renames python.exe to a random name, and executes a Python downloader script.
Step 5: The Python downloader fetches a second Python script from trindastal[.]com/loc8, which uses Cyrillic character substitution to obfuscate a ctypes shellcode loader.
Step 6: The shellcode loader decrypts XOR-encrypted shellcode (Shellcode #1) using a 16-byte key, allocates executable heap via HeapCreate/HeapAlloc, copies decrypted shellcode, and executes it.

Step 7: Shellcode #1 resolves APIs via PEB walk and djb2 hashing, downloads a second-stage RC4-encrypted shellcode from trindastal[.]com/v8, decrypts it (first 64 bytes as RC4 key), and executes it via ReplaceTextW hook abuse.
Step 8: Shellcode #2 is a reflective PE loader that maps an embedded PE (CastleLoader) into memory using direct syscalls (NtAllocateVirtualMemory) and rewrites the PEB to point to the embedded PE.

Step 9: CastleLoader communicates with its C2 using ChaCha20 encryption, receives task definitions, and executes them using one of 14 launch methods (e.g., process hollowing, APC injection, PowerShell).

Step 10: In the observed campaign, CastleLoader drops NetSupport RAT (via scheduled task) and CastleStealer (via APC injection). CastleStealer performs host fingerprinting, steals credentials, and exfiltrates data over TCP to 38.146.28[.]30:22989.
conclusions: The BackgroundFix campaign demonstrates a sophisticated multi-stage attack chain that leverages social engineering (fake background-removal tool), living-off-the-land binaries (finger.exe, curl, tar), and advanced evasion techniques (ReplaceTextW hook, direct syscalls, PEB rewriting). The chain is complex but each stage can be disrupted by basic security controls: disabling the Run dialog, blocking TCP port 79, and keeping browsers updated to use App-Bound Encryption. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: understanding this chain provides insights into modern adversary tradecraft and helps in designing more effective simulations.)

CastleLoader's use of a bring-your-own-interpreter (BYOI) approach with legitimate Python distribution is a notable evasion technique that bypasses application whitelisting. The loader's support for 14 launch methods and configurable C2 tasks makes it a versatile platform for delivering various payloads. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: this modular design can be emulated in custom C2 frameworks.)

CastleStealer is a relatively unexceptional stealer that relies on legacy DPAPI-then-AES extraction for Chromium credentials, making it vulnerable to App-Bound Encryption introduced in Chrome 127. However, its use of Restart Manager for locked files and process hollowing into PowerShell are noteworthy implementation details. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: the stealer's limitations highlight the importance of keeping browsers updated; its techniques can be adopted or defended against.)

The article provides actionable IOCs and detection recommendations, including a YARA rule for CastleStealer. The authors emphasize that the screenshot exfiltration pipeline (uncompressed BMPs over HTTPS) is a network-side detection signal. (🟢 Relevant to Red Teaming: these IOCs can be used to test detection capabilities in a lab environment.)
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your Red Teaming and programming interests. It provides a deep technical analysis of a real-world multi-stage malware campaign, covering social engineering, living-off-the-land binaries, Python-based downloaders, shellcode with advanced evasion techniques (ReplaceTextW hook, direct syscalls, PEB manipulation), and a reflective PE loader. As a programmer, you can study the code snippets (e.g., djb2 hashing, RC4 decryption, API hashing) and understand how to implement similar techniques in your own tools. The article also highlights weaknesses in the adversary's tooling (e.g., the typo in regsvr32.exe) that you can exploit in defensive exercises. For your homelab, you can set up detection rules for the IOCs provided and test your EDR's ability to detect the techniques described. As a student, the article demonstrates how to write a thorough technical analysis, which can serve as a model for your own research reports.
extra_advice: Consider building a lab environment to emulate the BackgroundFix attack chain. Use the provided IOCs to simulate the C2 traffic and test your detection capabilities. Additionally, experiment with implementing the ReplaceTextW hook abuse technique in a custom shellcode loader to understand its evasion potential. For your Red Teaming toolkit, incorporate the use of finger.exe as a LOLBin for initial payload delivery, and explore the BYOI approach with Python or other interpreters to bypass application whitelisting.
